title: Design Video Streaming Service Like Netflix

transcript.text: Design Video Streaming Service Like Netflix

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — encoding/CDN distribution, the metadata catalog at scale, and search via a change-data-capture pipeline — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension in Netflix is this: it's a **read-heavy, watch-heavy** system where the expensive thing isn't the database — it's moving petabytes of video bytes to tens of millions of screens at low latency without buffering, while keeping a huge searchable catalog fresh. So almost every decision bends toward **caching bytes close to users (CDN)** and **keeping the metadata/search layer fast and eventually-consistent**, not toward transactional correctness.

> 💬 "The hard part of Netflix isn't storing a movie once — it's serving the same movie to 50 million people on a Friday night without the database or the network melting."

I'll target a **Google/Amazon-style senior bar** with Amazon's cost-and-operations lens featured (this is an Amazon SDE-3 loop) — tell me if you'd rather I tilt elsewhere and I'll re-emphasize.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**FR1 — Upload & ingest a video (studio/content side).** A content owner uploads a high-bitrate master file; the system ingests, validates, and stores it.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A studio hands us one giant, pristine copy of a movie, and we accept it and stash it safely so we can process it."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** a master file can be 50–100 GB+; uploads fail halfway, so we need resumable, chunked ingestion and durable storage before any processing starts.

**FR2 — Encode/transcode into multiple formats & bitrates.** Convert the master into many renditions (resolutions, bitrates, codecs) for adaptive streaming.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We chop the one big file into lots of smaller quality versions — 4K, 1080p, 480p — so a phone on slow wifi and a TV on fiber each get the right one."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** transcoding is CPU-massive and embarrassingly parallel; one movie = thousands of independent chunk-encode jobs that must be orchestrated, retried, and reassembled correctly.

**FR3 — Stream video to viewers (the watch path).** A user presses play and gets smooth, adaptive playback that adjusts quality to their bandwidth.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I hit play and the movie starts in a second or two and never buffers, even if my wifi gets worse mid-scene."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is the petabyte-scale, latency-critical path — you cannot serve this from a central datacenter; bytes must already be sitting on a server near the user.

**FR4 — Browse & view catalog/metadata.** Users see the home page, rows, artwork, descriptions, and a title detail page.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The home screen full of posters and rows loads instantly, with the right title, cast, and 'resume watching' info."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the catalog is tens of millions of rows read by hundreds of millions of users — read fan-out is enormous and must be cached, not hit the source DB.

**FR5 — Search the catalog.** Users type a query and get ranked, typo-tolerant, near-real-time results.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I type 'stranger' and instantly see Stranger Things, even with a typo, and a title added this morning shows up too."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** full-text ranked search across millions of titles in <100ms is not something a relational DB does well, and the index must stay fresh as the catalog changes — that's the CDC pipeline.

**FR6 — Track playback progress / resume.** Remember where each user stopped on each title, across devices.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I stop a show on my phone and pick up at the exact same second on my TV."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** it's a high-write, per-user, per-title stream of heartbeats — huge write volume that must be cheap, not strongly consistent.

**Below the line (out of scope for this pass):** recommendations/personalization ML, billing & subscriptions, DRM key management internals, user authentication signup, social/sharing, ads. I'll mention DRM and recs where they touch the design but won't design them fully unless you want.

### Non-Functional

**NFR1 — High availability on the watch path: 99.99% ("four nines").**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Four nines means the service is unavailable less than about 52 minutes per year."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** "If play fails during prime time, millions of people see a spinner at once — that's the headline-news, mass-cancellation failure."

**NFR2 — Low startup latency: video starts in under ~2 seconds (p99), rebuffer ratio < 0.5%.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Press play, picture in two seconds, and almost never freezes to rebuffer."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** "Every extra second of startup measurably increases the chance the viewer abandons the title — slow start directly loses watch time."

**NFR3 — Massive read scalability: hundreds of millions of users, petabytes/day of egress.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "On a Friday night tens of millions stream at once and the system just absorbs it."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** "A popular launch (new season drop) becomes a thundering herd that overwhelms origin and the catalog DB, taking the whole site down."

**NFR4 — Durability of masters and catalog: eleven nines (S3-class), no data loss.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We must never lose a studio master or the catalog — losing a master means re-ingesting from the studio, which may be impossible."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** "Lose a master and a title can disappear permanently; lose catalog data and the whole UI is wrong."

**NFR5 — Freshness of search/catalog: new or changed titles visible within seconds to a couple minutes.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When we add or edit a title, it shows up in search and browse within a minute, not the next day."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** "A launch goes live but isn't searchable — users can't find the thing we just promoted."

**NFR6 — Global low latency: viewers worldwide get a nearby server.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A viewer in Mumbai and a viewer in Texas both get fast playback from a server near them."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** "Serving a viewer in India from a US datacenter means hundreds of ms of latency and constant rebuffering."

**NFR7 — Cost efficiency (Amazon lens): egress/CDN is the dominant cost; minimize it.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The single biggest bill is the bandwidth of sending video bytes — design to send each byte from the cheapest, closest place."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** "Naively serving all video from cloud egress would cost an order of magnitude more than the entire rest of the system combined."

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** read-your-own-writes on playback progress is *intentionally* weak (resume position can lag a few seconds across devices) — I'll call that out rather than over-engineer it. DRM is treated as a black box.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So Netflix is really two systems glued together. One is a content pipeline — studios upload masters, we transcode them into every quality and codec, and we push those bytes out to edge servers around the world. The other is a viewing experience — browse, search, press play, resume. The functional list is upload, encode, stream, browse, search, and resume. Non-functionally I care most about four-nines availability on play, sub-two-second startup, and global scale — and because this is Amazon, I'm going to keep cost, specifically CDN egress, front of mind the whole time, because that's where the money goes."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "How many monthly and daily active users are we designing for, and what's the concurrent-stream peak?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking (plain words):** "The peak number of people watching at the same moment is what sizes the CDN and origin — that single number drives most of the design."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If YES it's hundreds of millions MAU with tens of millions concurrent → must build/lease an edge CDN (Open Connect style) and can't rely solely on a 3rd-party CDN economically. If NO it's a few million → a commercial CDN (CloudFront/Akamai) is fine and I skip the custom appliance story.
- **Assumption:** ~300M MAU, ~100M DAU, peak ~50M concurrent streams. Big enough to justify a custom edge tier.

**Q2 — "Is the catalog mostly long-form on-demand video, or do we also need live streaming?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Live changes everything — you can't pre-encode and pre-position content that's being created right now."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If YES live too → need real-time/low-latency packaging, no pre-warming, different latency budget. If NO, VOD only → pre-encode everything and pre-push to edges (the easy, high-quality case).
- **Assumption:** VOD only this pass. I'll note where live would diverge.

**Q3 — "What's the read:write ratio — how watch-heavy vs. upload-heavy is this?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If reads dominate by thousands to one, I optimize entirely for caching reads and treat writes as a background pipeline."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If reads vastly dominate (expected) → CDN + cache everything, source DB barely touched. If writes were heavy → I'd need a very different ingest-scaled architecture.
- **Assumption:** Reads dominate by ~10,000:1+ over catalog writes; playback heartbeats are the main write volume.

**Q4 — "How fresh must search and the catalog be after an edit — seconds, minutes, or is overnight fine?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Freshness decides whether I need a real-time CDC pipeline to the search index or can rebuild it on a batch schedule."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If seconds–minutes → CDC (Change Data Capture) streaming catalog edits into OpenSearch. If overnight is fine → a nightly batch reindex, far simpler.
- **Assumption:** Seconds-to-a-minute freshness required → CDC pipeline (this is one of the requested focus areas).

**Q5 — "Do we need device-aware encoding (many codecs/resolutions) or one universal format?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "The number of renditions multiplies storage and transcode cost — it's the main driver of the encoding fleet size."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If many (H.264, HEVC, AV1 × multiple resolutions) → big parallel transcode fleet + per-title encoding. If one universal → tiny pipeline.
- **Assumption:** Many renditions, adaptive bitrate (ABR), with per-title/per-scene optimization.

**Q6 — "What consistency does playback progress need — exact across devices instantly, or eventually fine?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If 'resume' can be a few seconds stale across devices, I can make it a cheap, eventually-consistent, high-write KV store instead of a strongly-consistent DB."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If eventual is fine → Cassandra/DynamoDB-style KV, fire-and-forget heartbeats. If must be exact-instant → strong consistency, much more expensive.
- **Assumption:** Eventual consistency for progress; a few seconds of staleness is acceptable.

**Q7 — "Are we global from day one with data-residency constraints (GDPR etc.)?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Residency rules change where user data lives and whether I can do single-region writes."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If strict residency → regionalize user data stores. If not → simpler global replication.
- **Assumption:** Global, multi-region; catalog replicated everywhere, user data regional with replication.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — VOD, ~50M concurrent, reads dominate, CDC-fresh search, eventual progress. Before I size it, anything there you'd change, or should I keep going?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll only compute the few numbers that actually drive design choices. Glossing terms as I go: **QPS** — queries per second, how many requests hit a server each second. **Egress** — bytes sent *out* to users, which is what CDNs and clouds charge for. **ABR** — adaptive bitrate, the technique of switching quality on the fly.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak concurrent streams | 100M DAU; assume ~50% watch at evening peak → 50M concurrent | ~50M | "fifty million streams at once" | Sizes the entire CDN edge tier |
| Peak video egress | 50M streams × ~5 Mbps avg bitrate = 250M Mbps = 250 Tbps | ~250 Tbps | "about 250 terabits a second" | This is why we own the edge CDN, not rent it |
| Daily egress volume | 50M × 5 Mbps × 2 hrs × 3600s ÷ 8 = ~225 PB/day at peak hrs (and far more across the day) | ~hundreds of PB/day | "hundreds of petabytes a day" | Dominant cost line → push bytes to ISP edge |
| Catalog browse QPS | 100M DAU × ~20 home/detail loads/day ÷ 86,400 ≈ 23K/s; peak ×5 ≈ ~115K/s | ~120K QPS | "north of a hundred thousand metadata reads a second" | Must be cache/CDN-served, never raw DB |
| Catalog DB miss QPS | At 99% cache hit ratio: 120K × 1% = 1.2K/s to source DB; cold cache (0% hit) = 120K/s | ~1.2K QPS (warm) | "about twelve hundred reads a second reach the DB" | A few read replicas suffice; cold cache is the danger |
| Playback heartbeat writes | 50M concurrent × 1 heartbeat / 30s = ~1.7M writes/s | ~1.7M writes/s | "well over a million progress writes a second" | Forces a cheap, horizontally-sharded KV store, not SQL |
| Master storage | 50K titles × ~avg 5 renditions × ~5 GB + masters ~100GB each = low single-digit PB; with replication ~10–20 PB | ~tens of PB | "tens of petabytes of encoded video" | Object storage (S3-class), cheap & durable; not the cost driver |
| Catalog metadata size | 50M catalog rows × ~5 KB = ~250 GB; with indexes/replicas ~1 TB | ~1 TB | "a terabyte of metadata" | Trivially fits one DB + replicas; not a scaling problem |

**The ONE number that forces a design choice:** **~250 Tbps of peak egress.** A commercial CDN at that scale is economically and physically prohibitive — that's the flip threshold that justifies building an **Open Connect-style** edge CDN (our own caching appliances placed inside ISP networks). The metadata side, by contrast, is tiny — a terabyte — so I will *not* spend design effort sharding the catalog DB; I'll spend it on the byte-delivery path.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that matters is egress: about 250 terabits a second at peak. That's so large that renting a commercial CDN isn't viable on cost or capacity, so I'll build our own edge caching tier inside ISPs. Meanwhile the metadata is only about a terabyte and the source DB sees barely a thousand reads a second once cached — so I'm not going to waste time scaling the database. I'll spend my whole budget on getting video bytes close to users cheaply, and on a write path that can absorb over a million playback heartbeats a second."

## 4. Core Entities

> 🎙️ I'd start by naming the key entities and why each one exists; these are a first draft and I'll add full field definitions in the data model in §7.

**Title (a.k.a. Video / Content)** — the catalog unit: a movie or an episode, owning its metadata (name, synopsis, cast, artwork refs, ratings, availability windows).
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of a Title like a product page in a store catalog — it describes the thing, but it isn't the thing itself."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "They'll ask 'how does a Title relate to the actual video bytes?' → answer: a Title points to many Renditions (encoded files); the Title is metadata, the Renditions are the playable media."

**Rendition (Encoded Asset)** — one specific encoded version of a Title: a (codec, resolution, bitrate) variant, stored as segmented media files plus a manifest.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like the same song offered as MP3-320, MP3-128, and FLAC — same content, different quality/format for different needs."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "'How does the player know which rendition to fetch?' → it downloads a manifest listing all renditions and segments, then ABR picks the right one per segment based on bandwidth."

**User** — the account/profile watching content; owns preferences, region, device list, and entitlement.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "The membership card — it says who you are, where you are, and what you're allowed to watch."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "'How do you enforce who can watch what?' → entitlement + region checks at the play-authorization step before issuing a signed playback URL."

**PlaybackProgress (ViewingPosition)** — per (user, title) record of how far the user has watched; high write volume.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "A bookmark that quietly moves itself every few seconds as you read."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "'A million writes a second — how?' → it's eventually consistent, sharded by user_id in a wide-column KV store; we don't need transactions for a bookmark."

**EncodingJob** — the unit of work in the transcode pipeline: tracks a Title (or chunk) through ingest → transcode → package → publish, with status and retries.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "A work order on an assembly line — it follows the movie through each station and records what's done."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "'What if a chunk fails?' → each chunk is an idempotent sub-job; we retry just that chunk, not the whole movie."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are Title and Rendition on the content side — a Title is the metadata, a Rendition is one encoded quality of it — plus User, PlaybackProgress, and EncodingJob. The one I'd flag as most important and most surprising is the split between Title and Rendition: people conflate 'the movie' with 'the file,' but separating metadata from the many encoded variants is what lets us scale browse cheaply and serve the right bytes per device. I'll add full field tables in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

I'll walk the functional requirements and define endpoints ~1:1. Default REST; the watch path uses signed URLs + a manifest, not a giant JSON blob.

| Method + Path | Auth | Satisfies |
|---|---|---|
| POST /v1/ingest/uploads | Studio token | FR1 upload |
| GET /v1/titles/{id} | Session/JWT | FR4 browse |
| GET /v1/home | Session/JWT | FR4 browse |
| GET /v1/search?q= | Session/JWT | FR5 search |
| POST /v1/titles/{id}/play | Session/JWT | FR3 stream (authorize) |
| PUT /v1/progress/{titleId} | Session/JWT | FR6 resume |

**POST /v1/ingest/uploads** — POST because it *creates* new ingest state. Returns a resumable, chunked upload URL.
- Key request fields: `titleId: string` — links bytes to the catalog entry; `checksum: string` — verify integrity after a multi-GB upload; `parts: int` — for multipart/resumable upload.
- Absent on purpose: no raw bytes in this call — the response hands back pre-signed multipart URLs so the giant file goes straight to object storage, never through our API server.
```
// Request
POST /v1/ingest/uploads
{ "titleId": "ttl_889", "checksum": "sha256:9af...", "parts": 920 }

// Response 200
{ "uploadId": "up_551", "partUrls": ["https://obj.store/...part1", "..."], "status": "AWAITING_PARTS" }
```
- **Key design decision:** resumable multipart upload — a 100 GB master will hit network blips, so we must resume from the last good part, not restart.

**GET /v1/titles/{id}** — GET, read-only, fully cacheable at CDN/edge.
- Response fields: `renditionsManifestUrl` — where the player gets the media manifest; `artwork[]` — CDN URLs; `availability` — region/window so client hides unavailable titles.
```
// GET /v1/titles/ttl_889
// Response 200
{ "id": "ttl_889", "name": "Stranger Things", "synopsis": "...", "artwork": ["https://cdn/.../p.jpg"], "manifestUrl": "https://cdn/.../ttl_889.mpd", "availability": {"region": "US", "until": "2027-01-01"} }
```
- **Key design decision:** this response is cached aggressively (CDN + edge) with a short TTL; the source DB is almost never hit.

**GET /v1/search?q=** — GET; query, not mutation. Hits OpenSearch, not the source DB.
- Request fields: `q: string` — the typed query; `limit: int`; optional `region` (server fills from session — client can't widen its own entitlement).
```
// GET /v1/search?q=strangr&limit=10
// Response 200
{ "results": [ {"id":"ttl_889","name":"Stranger Things","score":18.2,"artwork":"https://cdn/.../p.jpg"} ] }
```
- **Key design decision:** typo tolerance + ranking come from the search engine; region filter is server-applied so a client can't search content it isn't entitled to.

**POST /v1/titles/{id}/play** — POST because it *creates* a short-lived, authorized playback session (and logs a play event); it's not a pure read.
- Request fields: `deviceProfile: string` — capabilities (codecs/DRM) so we return a compatible manifest; `titleId` (path). Client does NOT send entitlement or region — server derives both from the session, so no one can forge access.
```
// POST /v1/titles/ttl_889/play
{ "deviceProfile": "androidtv_hevc_widevine" }

// Response 200
{ "manifestUrl": "https://cdn/.../ttl_889.mpd?token=eyJ...", "drmLicenseUrl": "https://drm/.../lic", "expiresIn": 14400 }
```
- **Key design decision:** the manifest URL is **signed/tokenized** and short-lived — that's how we authorize a specific user/device for a specific title without putting auth in the byte path; the CDN validates the token.

**PUT /v1/progress/{titleId}** — PUT replaces the current position for (user, title); idempotent by nature.
- Request fields: `positionSec: int` — where playback is now; `deviceId`. Idempotency is free here: the same heartbeat applied twice yields the same position (last-write-wins).
```
// PUT /v1/progress/ttl_889
{ "positionSec": 1432, "deviceId": "dev_77" }
// Response 204 No Content
```
- **Key design decision:** fire-and-forget, eventually consistent; we accept it and queue it — we never block playback on a progress write.

> 💬 "Notice the price/entitlement and region are never in the request — the server derives them from the session, so a client can't grant itself access by editing the body."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The APIs split cleanly. Ingest is a resumable multipart upload so a hundred-gig master can survive network drops. Browse and title detail are plain cacheable GETs. Search is a GET that hits the search engine, not the database, with the region filter applied server-side. Play is a POST that returns a *signed, expiring* manifest URL plus a DRM license URL — that token is how the CDN authorizes a user for a title without auth living in the byte path. And progress is a fire-and-forget PUT — last-write-wins, never blocks playback."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Upload & ingest a video"

A studio pushes a huge master; we must accept it durably before any processing. New components: an **Ingest Service** (issues resumable upload URLs, validates) and **Object Storage** (the durable landing zone).

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest Service | A small API that hands out pre-signed multipart upload URLs and validates checksums | Keeps multi-GB bytes off our app servers — they go straight to storage | Not "upload through the API server" — would saturate app servers and memory on 100 GB files | Client must implement multipart/resume logic |
| Object Storage (S3-class) | A durable blob store that keeps files across many disks/datacenters | Eleven-nines durability for irreplaceable masters at low $/GB | Not a filesystem/NAS (no global durability); not a DB (blobs aren't rows) | Higher per-request latency than local disk (irrelevant here) |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Pre-signed multipart upload** — a temporary URL that lets a client upload a file in many independent parts directly to storage. Here it lets a 100 GB master upload resume after a network drop without touching our servers.
> **Object Storage (S3-class)** — a service that stores arbitrary files ("objects") redundantly across many machines. Here it's the durable home for masters and encoded segments.

**Diagram — Ingest flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Studio[Studio uploader]
    Studio -->|"1: request upload"| Ingest[Ingest Service]
    Ingest -->|"2: pre-signed URLs"| Studio
    Studio -.->|"3: upload parts"| OS[(Object Storage)]
    Ingest -->|"4: register master, emit event"| Q[[Ingest Queue]]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Studio client
    class Ingest svc
    class Q queue
    class OS db
```

**Numbered narration:**
1. Studio requests an upload — the Ingest Service owns issuing pre-signed multipart URLs because it's the only component that should know storage layout. State change: an EncodingJob row is created as `AWAITING_PARTS`. What could go wrong: duplicate request → idempotent on titleId+checksum, returns the same uploadId.
2. Ingest returns the URLs. No state change. If the title doesn't exist → 404 before any bytes move.
3. Studio uploads parts directly to Object Storage. State: parts land durably. A failed part is retried individually (resume), not the whole file.
4. On completion + checksum match, Ingest registers the master and emits an event to the queue to kick off encoding. If checksum mismatches → reject and ask for re-upload; the bad object is GC'd.

> 💬 "The bytes never touch my API server — they go straight to durable storage, and I only orchestrate."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "For ingest, the studio asks my Ingest Service for a resumable, pre-signed multipart upload URL, then streams the hundred-gig master directly into object storage — never through my servers. I validate a checksum at the end so I know the file's intact, and then I drop an event on a queue that says 'this master is ready to encode.' The trade-off is the client has to do multipart upload logic, but that's standard, and it's what makes a hundred-gig upload survivable."

### 6.2 "Encode/transcode into multiple formats & bitrates"

This is the parallel-compute heart of the pipeline. New components: an **Encoding Orchestrator** and a large **Transcode Worker Fleet**, fed by the queue.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Encoding Orchestrator | A controller that splits a movie into chunks and fans out encode jobs, then reassembles | One movie = thousands of independent chunk jobs; need orchestration + retry tracking | Not a single big transcode process (no parallelism, hours per title); not manual | Added coordination complexity |
| Transcode Worker Fleet | A horizontally-scaled pool of CPU/GPU workers each encoding one chunk-rendition | Embarrassingly parallel → linear speedup; spot/elastic for cost | Not fixed servers (idle cost); not a single GPU box (too slow) | Workers can die mid-chunk → must be idempotent |
| Message Queue | A durable buffer holding encode jobs until workers pick them up | Decouples bursty ingest from worker capacity; nothing lost if workers lag | Not direct RPC (back-pressure/loss); not a DB-as-queue (poor throughput) | At-least-once delivery → jobs must be idempotent |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Transcode/encode** — converting video from one format/quality to others. Here it produces every rendition (4K/1080p/480p, H.264/HEVC/AV1) from one master.
> **Chunked (parallel) encoding** — splitting the video timeline into short segments encoded independently in parallel. Here it turns a multi-hour job into minutes by using thousands of workers.
> **Message Queue (e.g., SQS/Kafka)** — a durable log/buffer of work items. Here it holds encode jobs so a burst of uploads doesn't overwhelm the worker fleet and nothing is lost.

**Diagram — Encoding pipeline**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[[Ingest Queue]]
    Q -->|"1: master ready"| Orch[Encoding Orchestrator]
    Orch -->|"2: fan-out chunk jobs"| JQ[[Chunk Job Queue]]
    JQ -->|"3: pull chunk"| W[Transcode Workers]
    W -->|"4: write segments"| OS[(Object Storage)]
    W -->|"5: chunk done"| Orch
    Orch -->|"6: package + manifest, publish"| OS
    Orch -.->|"7: title encoded event"| CDC[[Catalog Event]]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Orch,W svc
    class Q,JQ,CDC queue
    class OS db
```
↳ reuses existing: Object Storage, Ingest Queue (from §6.1)

**Numbered narration:**
1. Orchestrator consumes "master ready." It owns the title-level workflow. State: EncodingJob → `ENCODING`. If orchestrator crashes → workflow state is persisted, a new one resumes.
2. It fans out one job per (chunk × rendition) onto the chunk queue. State: N sub-jobs `PENDING`. Idempotent by (titleId, chunkId, rendition).
3. Workers pull chunks. Elastic pool scales with queue depth. If a worker dies mid-chunk → the message reappears (visibility timeout) and another worker redoes just that chunk.
4. Workers write encoded segments to Object Storage. Writing the same chunk twice is safe (same key, overwrite).
5. Worker reports completion. State: sub-job `DONE`.
6. When all chunks for a rendition are done, Orchestrator packages segments + writes the **manifest** (the index the player reads). State: Title → `PUBLISHED`.
7. Orchestrator emits a "title encoded/updated" event — this feeds the catalog/CDC pipeline (§6.5) and triggers CDN pre-positioning.

> 💬 "Each chunk is an independent, idempotent job — so a movie encodes in minutes and a dead worker only costs one chunk."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Encoding is embarrassingly parallel, so I split each movie into short chunks and fan out thousands of independent encode jobs onto a queue. An elastic fleet of workers — cheap spot instances — each grab one chunk, encode it into the renditions, and write segments to object storage. Because every chunk job is idempotent, a worker dying just means one chunk gets retried, not the whole movie. When all chunks finish, the orchestrator packages them and writes the manifest, marks the title published, and fires an event that tells the catalog and the CDN the title's ready."

### 6.3 "Stream video to viewers"

The latency-critical, petabyte path. New components: a **CDN / Edge tier** (Open Connect-style appliances) and a **Playback Authorization Service**. The player uses **ABR** over the manifest.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Edge CDN (Open Connect) | Caching servers placed inside ISPs holding popular segments near users | 250 Tbps egress is impossible/too costly from central origin; ISP-local bytes are cheapest+fastest | Not commercial CDN only (cost prohibitive at this scale); not central origin (latency, bandwidth) | We must operate hardware + pre-position content |
| Playback Auth Service | Validates entitlement/region, issues a signed, expiring manifest token | Keeps auth out of the byte path while still controlling access | Not auth on every segment request (too slow); not no-auth (piracy) | Token expiry must be tuned vs. revocation |
| Origin Storage | The authoritative copy of all segments (object storage) behind the edge | Edge cache miss must fall back somewhere durable | Not edge-only (cache isn't durable) | Origin egress on cold content |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a fleet of geographically distributed caching servers. Here, our own Open Connect appliances inside ISPs serve video bytes from as close to the viewer as possible.
> **Open Connect appliance** — a Netflix-style caching box placed in an ISP's datacenter. Here it means the most popular content lives one network hop from the viewer, slashing cost and latency.
> **ABR (Adaptive Bitrate)** — the player's logic to switch quality per segment based on measured bandwidth. Here it's what prevents buffering when wifi degrades.
> **Manifest (MPD/M3U8)** — a small index file listing every rendition and segment URL. Here the player downloads it first, then fetches segments adaptively.

**Diagram — Watch path (hourglass)**
```mermaid

    Player[Player device]
    Player -->|"1: POST play"| Auth[Playback Auth Svc]
    Player -->|"4: GET segments"| Edge
    subgraph Control["⚡ Authorize"]
        Auth -->|"2: check entitlement"| Meta[(Metadata DB)]
        Auth -->|"3: signed manifest URL"| Player
    end
    subgraph Bytes["🎬 Byte path"]
        Edge[Edge CDN] -->|"5: cache miss"| Origin[(Origin Storage)]
    end
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Player client
    class Auth,Edge svc
    class Meta,Origin db
```
↳ reuses existing: Origin/Object Storage (from §6.1/6.2)

**Numbered narration:**
1. Player POSTs play. Auth Service owns access control. State: a play event logged. If unauthorized → 403, no token issued.
2. Auth checks entitlement/region against metadata. Cached, so ~1ms. If metadata down → fail open to cached entitlement or degrade gracefully.
3. Auth returns a signed, expiring manifest URL + DRM license URL. State: a short-lived grant exists. If token forged/expired → CDN rejects.
4. Player fetches the manifest then segments from the nearest Edge CDN, using ABR to pick quality. State: bytes served from cache. If a segment is missing → ABR retries a lower rendition.
5. On a cache miss, the edge pulls the segment from Origin, caches it, then serves it. State: edge now warm for the next viewer. If origin is far → first viewer sees slightly higher startup; pre-positioning avoids this for known-popular content.

> 💬 "Auth happens once and hands out a signed URL; after that the bytes flow straight from a server inside your ISP — no auth in the hot path."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When you press play, you hit my Playback Auth service once — it checks your entitlement and region and hands back a signed, expiring manifest URL plus a DRM license URL. From then on your player talks only to the nearest edge cache, which is literally a box inside your ISP, and uses adaptive bitrate to pick quality per segment. The vast majority of segments are already cached at the edge, so origin barely gets touched. The key insight: authorization is a one-time control-plane step, and the heavy byte path is pure cache reads close to the user."

### 6.4 "Browse & view catalog/metadata"

New components: a **Catalog Service**, a **Metadata DB**, and a **Read Cache** (plus CDN for artwork). Start minimal — one service, one DB, add cache because the read fan-out demands it.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catalog Service | Stateless API serving title/home/detail reads | Clean read API; horizontally scalable | Not direct DB access from clients (no caching/control) | Another hop |
| Metadata DB | Relational DB storing titles, renditions, availability | ~1 TB, relational relationships, easy queries; size is trivial | Not NoSQL (no relational need at 1 TB); not sharded (unnecessary) | Single-region writes (fine for catalog) |
| Read Cache (Redis) | In-memory store fronting the DB for hot titles | 120K read QPS must not hit DB; 99% hit ratio → ~1.2K to DB | Not DB-only (can't take the QPS); not CDN-only (dynamic per-user rows) | Cache staleness (short TTL) |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Read Cache (Redis)** — an in-memory key-value store that answers reads in microseconds. Here it absorbs the browse fan-out so the DB sees ~1% of traffic.
> **TTL (Time To Live)** — how long a cached value stays before expiring. Here a short TTL bounds how stale catalog data can be.

```mermaid
    Cache -.->|"3: miss"| DB[(Metadata DB)]
    Player -.->|"4: artwork"| CDN[Edge CDN]
```

**Numbered narration:**
1. Player GETs home/title. Catalog Service owns assembling the response. State: none (read). If service down → multi-instance behind LB.
2. It reads from cache first. ~99% hit. State: none.
3. On miss, read DB, populate cache. State: cache warmed. Thundering herd on a hot key (new launch) → single-flight/request-coalescing so only one request hits DB.
4. Artwork/images come from the CDN, not the API. State: none. If CDN down → fall back to origin images.

> 💬 "Browse is just cache reads — the source DB only sees the 1% of traffic that misses."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Browse is a stateless Catalog Service reading from Redis, which is fronting a small relational metadata DB. The whole catalog is only about a terabyte, so the DB itself is trivial — the challenge is the read fan-out, over a hundred thousand reads a second. The cache absorbs ~99% of that, so the DB sees barely a thousand. Artwork is served from the CDN, never my API. The one trap is a new-season launch creating a hot key — I solve that with request coalescing so only one request refills the cache."

### 6.5 "Search the catalog"

New components: a **Search Service**, **OpenSearch** (the reverse index), and a **CDC pipeline** keeping the index fresh from the Metadata DB.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OpenSearch | A search engine that pre-builds a word→title inverted index for ranked, typo-tolerant search | Need ranked full-text across millions of titles in <100ms — SQL can't | Not SQL LIKE (full scans, no ranking); not hand-rolled index (months, no relevance) | Lags DB by seconds (fine for display) |
| CDC pipeline | Streams every DB change (insert/update/delete) into the index in near-real-time | Freshness in seconds without batch reindex | Not nightly batch (too stale for launches); not dual-writes (consistency bugs) | Eventual consistency; pipeline must be reliable |
| Search Service | Stateless API translating queries into OpenSearch requests | Decouples client from the engine; applies region filter | Not client→OpenSearch direct (security, coupling) | Extra hop |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Inverted index (reverse index)** — a precomputed map from each word to the list of titles containing it. Here it's why search returns ranked matches in milliseconds instead of scanning rows.
> **OpenSearch** — an open-source search engine (Elasticsearch fork) providing full-text ranking and fuzzy matching. Here it powers typo-tolerant, ranked catalog search.
> **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — a technique that reads a database's change log and streams every row change as an event. Here it keeps the search index fresh within seconds of a catalog edit without dual writes.
> **Kafka** — a durable, replayable event log. Here it carries CDC change events to the indexer reliably and lets us replay to rebuild the index.

**Diagram — Search + CDC freshness**

```mermaid
    SS -->|"2: query index"| OSrch[(OpenSearch)]
    DB[(Metadata DB)] -.->|"3: change log"| CDC[[CDC - Kafka]]
    CDC -.->|"4: index updates"| Indexer[Indexer Worker]
    Indexer -.->|"5: upsert docs"| OSrch

    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Player client
    class SS,Indexer svc
    class CDC queue
    class OSrch,DB db
```
↳ reuses existing: Metadata DB (from §6.4)

**Numbered narration:**
1. Player GETs search. Search Service owns query handling + region filter. If down → multi-instance.
2. It queries OpenSearch's inverted index. ~<100ms, ranked + fuzzy. State: none. If OpenSearch slow → degrade to prefix match on cached popular titles.
3. Separately, every catalog change emits to the DB change log captured by CDC. State: change event durably in Kafka.
4. The Indexer consumes change events. At-least-once → upserts are idempotent by titleId.
5. Indexer upserts the document into OpenSearch. State: index reflects the change within seconds. If indexer lags → search is stale but never wrong; Kafka retains so we catch up/replay.

> 💬 "Search reads a separate fast index, and CDC keeps that index fresh from the DB without the app ever dual-writing."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Search hits OpenSearch, which holds an inverted index — a precomputed word-to-title map — giving ranked, typo-tolerant results in well under a hundred milliseconds, something a relational LIKE query simply can't do at this scale. To keep it fresh, I don't dual-write from the app, which is a classic source of drift. Instead I use Change Data Capture: I tail the metadata DB's change log into Kafka, and an indexer consumes those events and upserts documents into OpenSearch within seconds. The index can lag the DB by a few seconds, but it's never inconsistent, and because Kafka retains the events I can always replay to rebuild."

### 6.6 "Track playback progress / resume"

New components: a **Progress Service** and a high-write **Progress Store** (wide-column KV), buffered by a queue.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Progress Service | Stateless API accepting heartbeats, fire-and-forget | Must absorb ~1.7M writes/s without blocking playback | Not synchronous-to-DB (latency, coupling) | Eventual consistency |
| Progress Store (Cassandra/Dynamo) | A wide-column KV store sharded by user_id, optimized for huge write volume | Needs cheap, horizontally-scalable writes; LWW semantics fit a bookmark | Not SQL (can't take 1.7M writes/s economically); not Redis-only (not durable enough) | No multi-row transactions (not needed) |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Wide-column store (Cassandra/DynamoDB)** — a NoSQL DB optimized for massive write throughput, partitioned by key. Here it absorbs over a million progress writes a second, sharded by user_id.
> **Last-write-wins (LWW)** — conflict resolution where the newest timestamp simply overwrites. Here it's exactly right for a bookmark — the latest position is the truth.

**Diagram — Progress path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Player[Player device]
    Player -.->|"1: PUT heartbeat"| PS[Progress Service]
    PS -.->|"2: buffer"| PQ[[Progress Queue]]
    PQ -.->|"3: batch write"| Store[(Progress Store)]
    Player -->|"4: GET resume on load"| PS
    PS -->|"5: read position"| Store
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Player client
    class PS svc
    class PQ queue
    class Store db
```

**Numbered narration:**
1. Player PUTs a heartbeat every ~30s. Fire-and-forget; never blocks playback. If service down → client buffers and retries; losing a heartbeat just means resume is a few seconds off.
2. Service buffers to a queue. State: write queued. Smooths spikes.
3. Workers batch-write to the store. LWW by (user_id, title_id). Duplicate heartbeat → idempotent (same/older position ignored).
4. On app load, client GETs resume position.
5. Service reads the latest position. May lag a few seconds across devices — acceptable.

> 💬 "Resume is just a bookmark — I optimize for cheap, massive writes and accept a few seconds of staleness across devices."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Resume looks small but it's actually the biggest write load — over a million heartbeats a second. So I make it fire-and-forget: the player PUTs its position every thirty seconds, I buffer it and batch-write into a wide-column store like Cassandra, sharded by user, with last-write-wins. I deliberately don't make this strongly consistent — a bookmark being a couple seconds stale when you switch devices is completely fine, and that choice is what makes the write volume affordable."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Title | id (PK), name, synopsis, availability, region_windows | Relational (Postgres/Aurora) | id (not sharded; ~1 TB) | Strong (single-region writes) |
| Rendition | id (PK), title_id (FK), codec, resolution, bitrate, manifest_url | Relational | title_id | Strong |
| User/Entitlement | user_id (PK), region, plan, device_ids | Relational (regional) | user_id | Strong within region |
| PlaybackProgress | (user_id PARTITION, title_id), position_sec, updated_at | Wide-column (Cassandra/Dynamo) | user_id | Eventual (LWW) |
| EncodingJob | job_id (PK), title_id, chunk_id, status | Relational / workflow store | job_id | Strong |
| Search doc | title_id, name, cast, tokens | OpenSearch | routing by title_id | Eventual (CDC-fed) |
| Media segments | object key | Object/Origin Storage (S3-class) | key prefix | Strong-after-write, 11 nines durability |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ Object Storage (S3-class) — used for: masters, encoded segments, manifests, artwork**
1. **What it is:** "A service that stores files redundantly across many machines and datacenters, addressed by a key — extremely durable and cheap per gigabyte."
2. **Why we picked it — access pattern:** "We store tens of petabytes of irreplaceable masters and segments that are written once and read many times; we need eleven-nines durability and low $/GB, and we need the CDN origin to pull from it — that's exactly object storage's job."
3. **Considered instead:**
   - **Block storage / NAS** — no global durability, caps out in size, expensive to scale to petabytes.
   - **A database** — storing multi-GB blobs in a DB is an anti-pattern; terrible cost and performance.
   - **Edge-only** — caches aren't durable; we'd lose data on eviction.
4. **Trade-off:** "Higher per-object latency than local disk — irrelevant because the CDN caches hot bytes."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Masters and segments live in object storage — it's eleven-nines durable and cheap, and it's the origin the CDN pulls from. Putting petabytes of video in a database would be insane on cost and performance, so this is the obvious home."

**🗄️ Relational DB (Postgres/Aurora) — used for: titles, renditions, entitlements, jobs**
1. **What it is:** "A database that stores data in rows and columns with relationships and transactions."
2. **Why we picked it — access pattern:** "The catalog is only about a terabyte with real relationships — a title has many renditions, availability windows, etc. Relational queries and joins are natural, and at a terabyte there's zero scaling pressure."
3. **Considered instead:**
   - **NoSQL (Dynamo/Mongo)** — we don't need its scale here and we'd lose easy joins/transactions for catalog edits.
   - **Sharded SQL** — unnecessary complexity at 1 TB.
4. **Trade-off:** "Single-region writes — totally fine for a catalog that changes slowly; we replicate read replicas everywhere."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The catalog is relational and tiny — about a terabyte — so I use Postgres. I considered NoSQL but there's no scaling need, and I'd give up easy joins and transactions for catalog edits for no reason."

**🗄️ Read Cache (Redis) — used for: hot title/home reads, entitlement lookups**
1. **What it is:** "An in-memory key-value store that returns values in microseconds."
2. **Why we picked it — access pattern:** "Browse fan-out is ~120K QPS; the DB can't and shouldn't take that. A 99% hit ratio drops it to ~1.2K QPS at the DB."
3. **Considered instead:** **DB read replicas only** — would still need dozens of replicas and miss microsecond latency; **CDN only** — can't hold per-user dynamic rows.
4. **Trade-off:** "Short TTL staleness — acceptable for a catalog."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I put Redis in front of the metadata DB because browse traffic is over a hundred thousand reads a second. The cache eats 99% of it. The cost is a few seconds of staleness, which is nothing for a catalog."

**🗄️ Wide-column store (Cassandra/DynamoDB) — used for: playback progress**
1. **What it is:** "A NoSQL database built for enormous write throughput, partitioned by a key."
2. **Why we picked it — access pattern:** "~1.7M heartbeat writes/s, keyed by user — we need cheap, horizontally-scalable, last-write-wins writes, not transactions."
3. **Considered instead:** **SQL** — can't economically take 1.7M writes/s; **Redis** — fast but not durable enough as the system of record.
4. **Trade-off:** "No multi-row transactions — we don't need any for a bookmark."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Progress is a firehose of writes, so it goes in Cassandra sharded by user with last-write-wins. SQL would buckle on the write volume and I don't need transactions for a bookmark."

**🗄️ OpenSearch — used for: catalog search**
1. **What it is:** "A search engine that pre-builds an inverted index for ranked, fuzzy full-text search."
2. **Why we picked it — access pattern:** "Ranked, typo-tolerant search across millions of titles in <100ms — SQL LIKE can't rank or do it fast."
3. **Considered instead:** **SQL LIKE/full-text** — slow, no relevance ranking; **hand-rolled index** — months of work, no ranking.
4. **Trade-off:** "Eventually consistent with the DB (CDC-fed) — fine for search display."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Search lives in OpenSearch because we need ranked, typo-tolerant results in milliseconds, and it's kept fresh by CDC. A SQL LIKE would scan rows and couldn't rank."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catalog edit (add/update title) | Relational | Strong | Editors must see correct, committed state |
| Browse/title read | Redis → DB | Eventual (short TTL) | A few seconds stale is invisible to users |
| Search query | OpenSearch | Eventual (CDC) | Seconds of index lag is acceptable |
| Play authorization | Relational (cached) | Strong-ish (read entitlement) | Must not grant access wrongly |
| Progress write | Cassandra | Eventual (LWW) | A bookmark a few seconds stale is fine |
| Resume read (read-your-own-write) | Cassandra | Eventual | Cross-device resume may lag a few seconds — stated bound |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The storage tier is five stores doing five jobs. Object storage holds the petabytes of video — durable and cheap, and it's the CDN origin. A small Postgres holds the catalog, which is only a terabyte, so I didn't bother sharding it. Redis fronts it for the read fan-out. Progress goes in Cassandra because it's a write firehose with last-write-wins semantics — I considered SQL there but it can't take a million-plus writes a second economically. And search lives in OpenSearch, kept fresh by a CDC pipeline rather than dual writes."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the binding constraint — "250 Tbps of egress" for delivery, "freshness within seconds" for search — and reason forward from there.

### How do we encode one movie fast and reliably?

**#### Bad: Single monolithic transcode**
🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** "One machine takes the whole movie and converts it start to finish."
**Approach:** A single worker reads the master and encodes every rendition sequentially.
**Why people try this:** It's the simplest mental model — one input, one process.
**⚠️ What breaks:** A 2-hour 4K master × 6 renditions on one box takes many hours; if it crashes at 90% you restart from zero; you can't use a thousand idle machines. Throughput is capped at one machine.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** Needing to publish a title in minutes, and surviving worker crashes.

**#### Good: Chunked parallel encoding**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** "The monolith couldn't parallelize or recover — so I split the movie into independent chunks that encode in parallel and retry individually."
🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** "Cut the movie into short segments, hand each to a different machine, then stitch them back together."
**Approach:** Split timeline into ~2–10s chunks (GOP-aligned — a Group of Pictures is a self-contained run of frames you can encode independently). Fan out (chunk × rendition) jobs onto a queue; an elastic worker fleet encodes them; orchestrator reassembles + packages.
**Why reasonable:** Video is embarrassingly parallel along the timeline.
**⚠️ What breaks:** Naive splitting can cause quality seams at chunk boundaries; very long tail of stragglers (one slow chunk delays publish).
**🔁 What forces upgrade:** Wanting optimal quality-per-bitrate and no stragglers blocking publish.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[(Master)] -->|split| C[Chunk jobs]
    C -->|fan-out| WF[Worker fleet]
    WF -->|segments| OS[(Storage)]
    WF -->|reassemble| P[Package + manifest]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class WF,P,C svc
    class M,OS db
```

**#### Great: Per-title (per-shot) encoding + speculative stragglers**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** "Fixed bitrate ladders waste bits on simple scenes and starve complex ones, and stragglers delay publish — so I optimize the ladder per shot and duplicate slow chunks."
🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** "Analyze each scene and give it exactly the quality it needs — a cartoon needs fewer bits than an explosion — and if one chunk lags, run a backup copy in parallel."
**Approach:** Complexity-analyze each shot, choose an optimal bitrate ladder per title/scene (Netflix per-title/per-shot encoding); for stragglers, speculatively launch a duplicate and take whichever finishes first.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** Per-title encoding saves ~20% bitrate on average → on hundreds of PB/day of egress, ~20% is an enormous CDN cost cut, dwarfing the extra transcode CPU (one-time).
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Worker dies mid-chunk | One chunk delayed | Visibility timeout → re-queue, idempotent retry |
| Chunk straggler | Publish delayed | Speculative duplicate, take first finisher |
| Corrupt segment | Bad playback | Per-chunk checksum, re-encode |
| Orchestrator crash | Workflow stalls | Persisted workflow state, resume |
| Queue backlog | Slow publish | Autoscale workers on queue depth |

**🎙️ Script:** "I encode in parallel by chopping each movie into GOP-aligned chunks and fanning thousands of independent jobs to an elastic worker fleet, so a two-hour film publishes in minutes and a dead worker only costs one chunk. The senior move is per-title encoding: I analyze each scene's complexity and tune the bitrate ladder per shot, which cuts about 20% of bitrate — and since egress is my dominant cost, that 20% saves far more than the extra encode CPU costs. I also run speculative duplicates for straggler chunks so one slow machine never holds up a launch."
**🧠 If they ask** "why not just encode on the fly per request?": "Just-in-time transcoding burns CPU on every view and can't be cached as easily; pre-encoding once and serving cached segments billions of times is vastly cheaper at our read:write ratio."

### How do we serve 250 Tbps without melting (CDN distribution)?

**#### Bad: Serve all video from central origin**
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Every viewer downloads the movie straight from our datacenter."
**Approach:** Players hit origin storage directly.
**Why people try this:** One source of truth, simple.
**⚠️ What breaks:** 250 Tbps from a few datacenters is physically impossible and the egress bill is astronomical; viewers far from origin rebuffer constantly.
**🔁 What forces upgrade:** Any real scale and any global audience.

**#### Good: Commercial CDN (CloudFront/Akamai)**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** "Origin couldn't fan out — so I put a CDN of edge caches in front to serve bytes near users."
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Rent a network of caching servers around the world that hold copies near viewers."
**Approach:** Pull-through CDN caches popular segments at edge PoPs; origin only sees misses.
**Why reasonable:** Standard, no hardware to run.
**⚠️ What breaks:** At hundreds of PB/day, commercial egress pricing is enormous and you don't control placement inside ISPs; cross-ISP transit still congests.

**#### Great: Owned edge CDN inside ISPs (Open Connect) + pre-positioning**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** "Rented CDNs are too costly and not deep enough in the network — so I place my own caching appliances inside ISP datacenters and pre-push content before demand."
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Put our own caching boxes right inside the viewer's internet provider, and copy a new season onto them overnight before launch so it's already there when everyone presses play."
**Approach:** Deploy Open Connect Appliances (OCAs) in ISP PoPs and IXPs. A control plane predicts popularity (new releases, region) and **pre-positions** content to OCAs during off-peak. The client steering service routes each player to the best-filled, closest, healthiest OCA. ABR adapts quality.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** 250 Tbps from cloud egress at ~$0.02–0.08/GB would be on the order of >$1B/month; serving from ISP-local OCAs (peering/settlement-free) cuts this by ~90%+ — this is the single biggest cost lever in the system.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| OCA down | Viewers on it lose local cache | Steering reroutes to next-best OCA/region |
| Cache miss (cold title) | First viewer slower start | Pre-position popular content; fill from origin |
| Hot launch (thundering herd) | Origin spike on miss | Pre-warm before launch; tiered fill (OCA→regional→origin) |
| ISP link congested | Rebuffer | ABR drops bitrate; steer to alternate PoP |
| Token forged | Piracy | Signed expiring URLs validated at edge |

```mermaid
    Player[Player]
    Player -->|"1: steer"| Steer[Steering Svc]
    Steer -->|"2: best OCA"| Player
    Player -->|"3: segments"| OCA[ISP-local OCA]
    OCA -.->|"4: miss fill"| Reg[Regional cache]
    Reg -.->|"5: miss"| Origin[(Origin)]
    Ctrl[Control plane] -.->|"pre-position off-peak"| OCA
```

**🎙️ Script:** "The whole delivery design is dominated by one number — 250 terabits a second — so I don't rent a CDN, I build one inside ISPs: Open Connect appliances sitting in the provider's own datacenter, one hop from the viewer. The killer move is pre-positioning: I predict what's about to be popular, like a new season drop, and copy it onto those boxes overnight, so when everyone presses play at 8pm the bytes are already local and origin never sees the spike. A steering service routes each player to the closest healthy, well-filled box. This cuts egress cost by something like 90%, which is the biggest lever in the entire system."
**🧠 If they ask** "what about a brand-new viral title nobody predicted?": "Cold misses tier-fill OCA→regional→origin and warm the edge after the first viewer; it's a slightly slower first start for a few users, then it's local. Prediction handles the 99% that's predictable; tiered fill handles the tail."

### How do we keep search fresh (CDC pipeline)?

**#### Bad: Query the source DB for search**
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Search by scanning the catalog table with a LIKE."
**⚠️ What breaks:** SQL LIKE scans millions of rows, no ranking, no typo tolerance, slow — and search traffic competes with catalog reads.

**#### Good: Separate search index, dual-write**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** "SQL can't rank or fuzzy-match fast — so I add OpenSearch. But if the app writes to both DB and index..."
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Keep a separate search engine and update it whenever the app updates the database."
**Approach:** On each catalog write, app writes the DB and the index.
**⚠️ What breaks:** Dual writes drift — if the index write fails after the DB commit, search silently goes stale/wrong; no single source of truth; hard to backfill.
**🔁 What forces upgrade:** Need guaranteed eventual consistency and replayability.

**#### Great: CDC → Kafka → idempotent indexer**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** "Dual writes can partially fail and drift — so I make the database's own change log the single source of truth and stream it."
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Tap the database's built-in change log so every edit automatically flows to the search engine, and if anything lags we can replay it."
**Approach:** A CDC connector (e.g., Debezium) reads the DB write-ahead log (WAL — the durable change journal the DB writes before applying changes) and publishes row changes to Kafka. An indexer consumes, transforms, and upserts into OpenSearch, idempotent by title_id. Encoding-published events also flow in so "now streamable" is reflected.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** CDC lag target < few seconds; Kafka retention (e.g., 7 days) means a full index rebuild = replay from offset 0, no DB scan needed.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Indexer down | Search stale | Kafka retains; catch up on restart |
| OpenSearch node loss | Partial index | Replicas + replay to rebuild |
| Bad transform deploys | Wrong docs | Replay from offset after fix |
| Duplicate event | — | Idempotent upsert by title_id |
| DB WAL gap | Missed change | Periodic full snapshot reconcile |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DB[(Metadata DB)] -.->|"1: WAL changes"| CDC[CDC connector]
    CDC -.->|"2: events"| K[[Kafka]]
    K -.->|"3: consume"| IX[Indexer]
    IX -.->|"4: upsert"| OS[(OpenSearch)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class CDC,IX svc
    class K queue
    class DB,OS db
```

**🎙️ Script:** "For search freshness I avoid the classic dual-write trap, where the app writes both the DB and the index and they silently drift when one write fails. Instead I use Change Data Capture: a connector tails the database's write-ahead log and publishes every row change to Kafka, and an idempotent indexer upserts those into OpenSearch within seconds. The database is the single source of truth, the pipeline is replayable, and if I ever need to rebuild the whole index I just replay Kafka from the beginning — no scanning the live DB."
**🧠 If they ask** "isn't CDC eventually consistent — what if search is stale during a launch?": "Yes, lag is a few seconds, which is fine for display. For a launch I pre-index the title before flipping availability, so it's searchable the instant it goes live."

### How do we absorb 1.7M progress writes/s?
**#### Great: Fire-and-forget + queue + wide-column LWW** (covered in §6.6).
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** 1.7M writes/s × ~100 bytes ≈ 170 MB/s ingest; a single Cassandra node handles ~10–50K writes/s → ~50–100 nodes, sharded by user_id, scales linearly. SQL on one primary tops out ~tens of K writes/s → would need absurd sharding.
**🎙️ Script:** "Progress is the biggest write load, so it's fire-and-forget into a queue and batch-written to Cassandra with last-write-wins, sharded by user. It scales linearly with nodes, and I accept a few seconds of cross-device staleness because it's just a bookmark."
**🧠 If they ask** "what if a write is lost?": "We lose at most ~30 seconds of progress — the next heartbeat corrects it. Not worth strong consistency."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):**
- **Watch path: four nines (99.99%)** → ~52 minutes of downtime per year total. Achieved via the **Edge CDN** (thousands of OCAs — losing one is invisible), **multi-AZ** services (multi-AZ = multiple datacenters in one region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically), and **graceful degradation** (a stale cache still serves bytes).
- **Browse/search path: ~99.95%** → degrade to cached/popular results if OpenSearch or DB hiccup.
- **Money/entitlement path:** highest correctness, but it's a tiny fraction of traffic; cached entitlement keeps play working through brief DB blips.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table:**

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what user sees) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Edge OCA | Local cache gone | Steering reroutes to next-closest box; maybe slightly slower start | Auto re-route; OCA self-heals |
| Origin storage | Cache misses can't fill | Cached/popular content still plays; cold titles fail | Multi-region origin replicas |
| Metadata DB | Catalog writes blocked | Browse still works from Redis; editors blocked | Failover to replica (promote) |
| Redis cache | Reads hit DB | Slower browse; DB protected by request-coalescing | Cache rebuilds (warm) |
| OpenSearch | Search degraded | Fall back to prefix match on cached popular titles | Replicas + CDC replay |
| Kafka/CDC | Search stops updating | Search stale but correct; resumes on recovery | Retained log, catch up |
| Progress store | Resume stale | Playback unaffected; resume may be a bit off | Queue buffers; drain on recovery |
| Transcode fleet | Encoding stalls | Existing titles unaffected; new titles delayed | Autoscale/retry from queue |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class:**
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we can lose if it crashes right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before acknowledging.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until the service works again after a crash.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catalog/metadata | ≈0 | minutes | Synchronous replica + PITR backups; promote replica |
| Masters/segments | ≈0 | n/a (durable) | 11-nines object storage, multi-region replication |
| Search index | minutes (rebuildable) | minutes | Rebuild by replaying Kafka/CDC |
| Playback progress | ~30s (one heartbeat) | seconds | Multi-replica Cassandra; loss is one heartbeat |
| Encoding jobs | seconds | minutes | Persisted workflow; resume from queue |

**9D — Cost breakdown:**
- **#1 CDN egress (by far)** — every video byte served is charged/transported; at hundreds of PB/day this dwarfs everything. **Biggest optimization:** owned ISP-local OCAs + per-title encoding (~20% fewer bits) → the single largest cost cut.
- **#2 Transcode compute** — large but one-time per title; use spot instances.
- **#3 Object storage** — tens of PB, but cheap per GB; tier cold masters to colder storage classes.
- Metadata DB and Redis are rounding errors by comparison.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When things fail, the viewer mostly doesn't notice: if an edge box dies, steering just sends them to another one; if search degrades, they still get popular results; if the metadata DB blips, browse keeps serving from Redis and play keeps working off cached entitlement. The only thing a crash can lose is about thirty seconds of resume position, which self-corrects. And the cost story is one line: egress is everything, so owning the edge and shaving 20% of bitrate with per-title encoding is where the money is saved."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Owned edge CDN (Open Connect) vs. rented commercial CDN**
1. **Chose & why:** Build our own ISP-embedded caches — only way to serve 250 Tbps affordably and with control over placement.
2. **Gave up:** Massive operational burden — hardware, ISP relationships, a control/steering plane to run.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Like a soda company putting its own fridges in every corner store instead of paying a delivery truck per bottle."
4. **When this reverses:** "If scale were small (a few million users) or we were early-stage, rent a commercial CDN — the ops cost of owning isn't worth it below the egress threshold."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "At our egress, owning the edge cuts cost ~90% and controls quality; below a certain scale I'd just rent a CDN and skip the ops."

**🔀 Decision: Eventual consistency for search/progress vs. strong consistency**
1. **Chose & why:** Eventual (CDC-fed search, LWW progress) — enables the read scale and the 1.7M-writes/s firehose cheaply.
2. **Gave up:** Instant cross-device resume; search reflects edits a few seconds late.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "A bookmark that's a couple seconds behind when you switch devices — nobody cares."
4. **When this reverses:** "If we added something money-critical to these paths (e.g., pay-per-view rentals tied to position), I'd make that specific write strongly consistent."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Search and resume are eventually consistent on purpose — that's what makes the scale affordable, and the staleness is invisible."

**🔀 Decision: Pre-encode (VOD) vs. just-in-time transcoding**
1. **Chose & why:** Pre-encode once, serve cached segments billions of times — far cheaper at our read:write ratio.
2. **Gave up:** Storage for all renditions; slower time-to-publish for a new title.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Cook the meal once and reheat, instead of cooking fresh for every customer."
4. **When this reverses:** "For live streaming or a huge long-tail catalog where most titles are rarely watched, JIT transcoding of cold content saves storage."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I pre-encode because the same file is served millions of times; JIT would burn CPU per view. For live or a giant cold tail, I'd reconsider."

**🔀 Decision: Relational catalog (unsharded) vs. NoSQL/sharded**
1. **Chose & why:** It's only ~1 TB with real relationships — Postgres is simplest and correct.
2. **Gave up:** "Web-scale" write throughput we don't need.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Don't build a warehouse to store one filing cabinet."
4. **When this reverses:** "If catalog grew billions of rows or write-heavy (user-generated content), move to NoSQL/sharded."
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The catalog is tiny and relational, so I keep it in Postgres and spend my complexity budget on the byte path, not on sharding a terabyte."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'd defend hardest: owning the CDN, which only makes sense above a huge egress threshold; making search and progress eventually consistent to buy scale; pre-encoding instead of transcoding per view; and deliberately *not* sharding the tiny relational catalog. Each flips at a clear point — small scale flips the CDN, money-critical paths flip the consistency, live flips the encoding, and a billion-row catalog flips the database."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "What's the hardest part of this system, and how do you handle it?"** *(core)*
**Mechanism:** Delivering ~250 Tbps of egress globally with sub-2s startup. We solve it with an owned edge CDN of ISP-embedded caches plus predictive pre-positioning of soon-popular content during off-peak.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The hard part isn't storing movies — it's shipping the bytes to everyone at once, so we keep copies right inside people's internet providers and load new shows there before launch."
**💬 One-liner:** "The whole design bends around one number — 250 terabits a second — so we cache bytes one hop from the viewer."

**❓ "What happens when an edge cache (OCA) goes down mid-stream?"** *(failure)*
**Mechanism:** The steering service health-checks OCAs; on failure it reroutes the player to the next-closest healthy, well-filled box, and ABR may briefly dip quality during the switch. No central outage because there are thousands of boxes.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "If the nearby box dies, we just send you to another nearby box — you might see a one-second quality dip."
**💬 One-liner:** "Edge failure is a reroute, not an outage — that's the point of thousands of small caches."

**❓ "How do you handle a massive launch — everyone hits play at 8pm?"** *(scale / hot-spot)*
**Mechanism:** Pre-position the title onto OCAs before launch so origin sees almost no misses; tiered fill (OCA→regional→origin) absorbs the tail; catalog reads are coalesced so a hot key hits the DB once.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We copy the new season onto the local boxes the night before, so when everyone presses play it's already there."
**💬 One-liner:** "We beat the thundering herd by pre-warming the edge before demand arrives."

**❓ "Can search show a title that doesn't exist yet, or miss one that does?"** *(consistency)*
**Mechanism:** Search is CDC-fed and eventually consistent — lag is a few seconds. For launches we index the title before flipping its availability flag, so it's searchable the moment it's live; deletes propagate via the same change log.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Search can be a few seconds behind edits, but for a launch we add it to search first, so it's never missing when it goes live."
**💬 One-liner:** "Search is seconds-fresh via CDC, and we pre-index launches so they're never late."

**❓ "How do you stop someone sharing a playback URL or pirating content?"** *(security)*
**Mechanism:** Play returns a signed, short-lived manifest token tied to user/device; the CDN validates it at the edge, and content is DRM-encrypted with per-session license keys, so a copied URL expires fast and bytes are unplayable without a license.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Your play link is a temporary, personalized ticket, and the video itself is locked — a shared link stops working and the file is scrambled without the key."
**💬 One-liner:** "Signed expiring URLs plus DRM mean a leaked link is worthless in minutes."

**❓ "How do you prevent a client from watching content it isn't entitled to / in the wrong region?"** *(security/consistency)*
**Mechanism:** Entitlement and region are derived server-side from the session at the play step — never trusted from the request — and baked into the signed token the CDN enforces.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The server decides what you're allowed to watch from your account, not from anything your app sends."
**💬 One-liner:** "Access is server-derived and enforced at the edge — the client can't grant itself anything."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut cost?"** *(cost)*
**Mechanism:** CDN egress dominates — hundreds of PB/day. Biggest levers: ISP-local OCAs (settlement-free peering) and per-title/per-shot encoding that cuts ~20% of bitrate, which directly cuts egress.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Bandwidth is the giant bill; we cut it by serving from inside ISPs and by squeezing each movie to use fewer bits without looking worse."
**💬 One-liner:** "Egress is 90%+ of cost — own the edge and shave bitrate per title."

**❓ "How would you add live streaming later?"** *(extensibility)*
**Mechanism:** Reuse the player, CDN, and steering, but swap pre-encode for low-latency real-time packaging (chunked CMAF/LL-HLS), tighten the latency budget, and pre-position nothing — fill the edge as the stream is produced.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Live reuses the delivery network but can't pre-load content, so we package it in tiny pieces as it happens."
**💬 One-liner:** "Live keeps the CDN, drops pre-encoding, and adds real-time low-latency packaging."

**❓ "How would you add recommendations/personalized rows?"** *(extensibility)*
**Mechanism:** A separate recs/ML service composes the home response, reading the same catalog metadata; it's an additive read-path service feeding the Catalog Service's home assembly, cached per user with a short TTL.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Recommendations are just a smart service that picks which rows to show — it sits on top of the same catalog."
**💬 One-liner:** "Recs is an additive read service; it doesn't touch the byte path."

**❓ "Can two devices clobber each other's resume position?"** *(consistency/core)*
**Mechanism:** Last-write-wins by timestamp on (user_id, title_id). The newest heartbeat is the truth; a stale device's older write is ignored. Worst case is a few seconds of disagreement that self-heals on the next heartbeat.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Whichever device reported most recently wins — and it sorts itself out within seconds."
**💬 One-liner:** "Resume is last-write-wins — newest position is truth, no transactions needed."

**❓ "What if the metadata DB goes down during peak?"** *(failure)*
**Mechanism:** Browse keeps serving from Redis; play keeps working off cached entitlement; only catalog *edits* block. We fail over by promoting a synchronous replica (RPO≈0).
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Watching keeps working from cache; only the admin's ability to edit the catalog pauses while we fail over."
**💬 One-liner:** "The DB is off the hot path thanks to caching — an outage barely touches viewers."

**❓ "Why object storage for video instead of a database or filesystem?"** *(core/storage)*
**Mechanism:** Video is large, write-once-read-many blobs needing 11-nines durability at low $/GB and an HTTP origin for the CDN — exactly object storage's design point; DBs and NAS fail on cost, size, or durability.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Movies are huge files we write once and read forever — that's what object storage is built for."
**💬 One-liner:** "Petabytes of write-once blobs belong in object storage, not a database."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "Netflix is, in plain terms, a system that ships huge video files to hundreds of millions of screens at once while keeping a fresh, searchable catalog. The design splits into two halves. The read half is all about caching: browse and title pages come from Redis in front of a small relational catalog, search comes from OpenSearch, and — the heart of it — video bytes are served from our own caching boxes embedded inside ISPs, one hop from the viewer, with adaptive bitrate handling bad networks. The write half is a content pipeline: studios upload masters, we split each movie into chunks and encode it in parallel into every quality and codec — using per-title encoding to shave bitrate — then publish manifests to object storage. The two halves connect through events and a Change Data Capture pipeline: catalog changes stream into the search index within seconds, and encoding completion triggers the CDN to pre-position content before launch. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: at 250 terabits a second of egress, you win by getting bytes as close to the viewer as possible and never making the origin or the database do work the cache can do."
